clean_story:
**Pressing buttons. Believing outcomes.**

*Human trust in machines* describes how people rely on technology—from toasters to AI—to act predictably, even when they don’t fully understand how it works.

It starts with repetition: a vending machine drops a snack every time you insert coins. Over time, the brain shortcuts *"press button → get result"* into *"this works."* But trust isn’t blind. A 2018 MIT study found people distrust self-driving cars *unless* they see the vehicle hesitate at crosswalks—mimicking human caution. The more a machine mirrors familiar patterns (a robot’s "hesitant" pause, a chatbot’s typos), the safer it feels.

Yet trust fractures fast. In 2016, Microsoft’s Tay AI turned racist in hours because users gamed its learning rules. The lesson? Trust isn’t about perfection—it’s about *visible limits*. A thermostat fails openly (click, no heat); an algorithm fails silently (denied loan, no explanation). The first feels fixable. The second feels like betrayal.

Three related subjects are **machine transparency**, **automation bias**, and **why glitches feel personal**.
